sound_type: descending whinny grunt -- rapid, pig-like grunting notes that descend in pitch and accelerate; E=4
ecosystem: Freshwater marsh -- dense emergent vegetation (cattails, bulrushes, sedges), shallow water, muddy substrate. Obligate marsh specialist.
season: Summer breeder in western Washington. Some year-round. Peak vocalization spring through summer.

coordinates.description: Virginia Rail in freshwater marshes of Puget Sound lowlands. Cattails, bulrushes, shallow water.

taxonomy_note.description: FIRST RALLIDAE. The rail family enters. Laterally compressed ('thin as a rail'). Long-toed. Obligate marsh specialist. Secretive -- more heard than seen. Descending whinny grunt diagnostic.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Rallidae: first entry. Three in cluster (161, 162, 164).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Rallus from French 'rale' (rail). limicola from Latin 'limus' (mud) + 'cola' (dweller) -- mud-dweller.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Rallus -- the typical rails. Long-billed, laterally compressed, marsh specialists.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Gruiformes. First Gruiformes entry in the engine.
taxonomy_note.family_position: First Rallidae. Opens the rail family. Virginia Rail/Sora comparison pair at 161-162.

song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 1500-3000 Hz. The initial grunting notes carry the most energy.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Full whinny: 1-3 seconds. Series of 5-15 grunting notes.
song_metrics.note_structure: Rapid descending series of pig-like grunts: 'kid-ick kid-ick kid-ick-ick-ick-ick.' Descends in pitch and accelerates. Un-bird-like.
song_metrics.note_types: Descending whinny grunt (primary), ticket call 'kiddick' (contact), soft grunting/clicking (close range).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Single descending phrase repeated. Called from within dense marsh vegetation.
song_metrics.timbre: Grunting, pig-like, nasal. Distinctly un-bird-like. Immediately recognizable once learned.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Carries 100-300 meters across marsh. Produced from concealed position.
song_metrics.tempo: Accelerating within each whinny phrase. Moderate repetition rate between phrases.
song_metrics.context: Territorial announcement. Contact. Breeding season. Agitation. Dawn/dusk peak.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Slight. Western Washington birds at core of PNW range.
song_metrics.key_property: DESCENDING WHINNY GRUNT -- pig-like, accelerating, descending. FIRST RALLIDAE. E=4. v1.49.301.

plumage.overall: Rusty-brown upperparts, gray face, reddish-brown breast, barred flanks (black and white). Cryptic in marsh setting.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Long, slightly downcurved, reddish bill. Gray face contrasting with brown body. Laterally compressed silhouette.
plumage.flight_pattern: Weak, fluttering flight with dangling legs. Last resort. Prefers to run through marsh.

physical_description.overall: Length: 20-27 cm. Weight: ~85 g. Laterally compressed. Long-toed.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Long reddish bill. Gray face. Laterally compressed body. Barred flanks.
physical_description.field_id: Almost never seen. Detected by descending whinny grunt from marsh. If seen: small, laterally compressed bird with long reddish bill skulking through cattails.

nodes.title: Descending Whinny Grunt
nodes.description: Rapid descending series of pig-like grunts. 1000-4000 Hz. Accelerating. Carries 100-300m. Primary detection method. Called from within dense reeds.

nodes.title: Lateral Compression -- Thin as a Rail
nodes.description: Body laterally compressed to slip between marsh stems. The phrase 'thin as a rail' derives from the bird. Skulks at ground level. Runs rather than flies.

nodes.title: Long-Toed Adaptation
nodes.description: Disproportionately long toes spread weight across floating vegetation. 1.5x expected length. Walks on lily pads and cattail mats. Precocial chicks walk immediately.

nodes.title: First Rallidae
nodes.description: The rail family enters. Three entries: Virginia Rail (161), Sora (162), American Coot (164). All E=4. From invisible skulkers to visible swimmers.

cross_references.relationship: RALLIDAE COMPARISON PAIR! Virginia Rail (161) + Sora (162). Long bill vs. short bill. Grunt vs. musical whinny. Same marsh, different niches.
SECRETIVE TRANSITION! Mountain Quail: dense brush (Odontophoridae). Virginia Rail: dense marsh (Rallidae). Different habitats, same detection paradox.
RALLIDAE RANGE! Virginia Rail: invisible. Coot: visible. Same family, opposite strategies.
